skill.name: binary-re-static-analysis
skill.description: Analyze compiled binaries with static reverse-engineering workflows for radare2 and Ghidra. The skill guides function mapping, cross-reference review, decompilation, control-flow analysis, and structured reporting.
skill.summary: Guide static reverse engineering of binaries with radare2 and Ghidra workflows.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands are mostly true positives as tool-invocation guidance, but they are not evidence of malicious code in the skill. The skill legitimately supports reverse engineering with radare2, Ghidra, QEMU, Docker, shell loops, and temporary project files; this creates elevated operational risk when analyzing untrusted binaries. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden network beaconing, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External Analysis Tool Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs use of radare2, Ghidra headless, QEMU, Docker, shell loops, and comparison utilities. This is expected for binary reverse engineering, but agents following the guidance may run tools against attacker-supplied binaries and should require isolation and approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are visible throughout SKILL.md and are semantically central to the workflow. They are legitimate analysis commands, so the confidence is high for elevated operational risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Optional Native Execution of Untrusted Binary
medium_findings.description: The pre-analysis workflow includes native execution of a target binary after approval. The skill warns that approval is required, but native execution of unknown binaries can still compromise the analyst environment if isolation is not used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 38 directly shows running the target binary, and nearby text frames it as approval-gated. The risk is clear, although the safety warning reduces concern about malicious author intent.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access Is Limited to Analysis Artifacts
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings relate to checking a tool via standard streams and creating a temporary Ghidra project. These are normal local analysis actions and do not show unauthorized file harvesting or persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show ordinary local tool checks and temporary project setup. There is no evidence of sensitive file collection or writes outside expected analysis paths.

low_findings.title: Crypto and Network Terms Are Analysis Targets
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and network reconnaissance flags appear to come from the description and analysis patterns that tell users how to identify crypto and network behavior in binaries. No evidence found that the skill performs cryptography or scans networks itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are used as reverse-engineering topics, not as executable network or crypto operations. The confidence is slightly below very high because the skill can help analyze potentially malicious network-capable binaries.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples for Binary Analysis
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides many shell and tool commands. They should be run only in a controlled workspace with trusted paths and analyst approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit shell commands, including command substitution in a loop. They appear educational and task-relevant rather than hidden or malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Temporary Ghidra Project Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The Ghidra workflow writes an analysis project under a temporary directory and deletes it after processing. This is expected, but it is still local filesystem modification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is directly shown and bounded to a temporary project path. The command does not indicate persistence or sensitive data access.

content.user_title: Analyze Binaries with Static RE Tools
content.value_statement: Binary reverse engineering is difficult when functions, strings, imports, and data flow are scattered across tools. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for static analysis with radare2 and Ghidra.
actual_capabilities: Selects light or deep static analysis based on binary size and investigation goals.
Guides radare2 function enumeration, import mapping, string search, and cross-reference review.
Provides targeted decompilation workflows using r2ghidra and Ghidra headless.
Supports control-flow and call-graph extraction for selected functions.
Suggests patterns for tracing network calls, configuration access, and cryptographic routines.
Defines structured notes for functions, hypotheses, data flow, and unresolved questions.
limitations: It depends on external tools such as radare2, r2ghidra, Ghidra, Docker, QEMU, jq, and grep.
It does not replace dynamic analysis when behavior depends on runtime state or environment checks.
It may be slow or incomplete for very large, packed, obfuscated, or self-modifying binaries.
It requires analyst judgment before executing unknown binaries or installing analysis plugins.

use_cases.title: Map Unknown Firmware Binaries
use_cases.description: Identify functions, imports, strings, and architecture-specific analysis settings before deeper investigation.

use_cases.title: Triage Suspected Malware Statically
use_cases.description: Trace network APIs, configuration paths, crypto routines, and call chains without initially running the sample.

use_cases.title: Explain Decompiled Functions
use_cases.description: Use targeted decompilation and cross-references to explain what selected functions do and how they connect.

prompt_templates.title: Start Static Triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin analysis of an unknown binary after basic file triage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this binary statically. Start with architecture-aware function enumeration, imports, strings, and high-value functions to inspect next.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Investigate one function found during triage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use static analysis to explain the target function. Include callers, callees, referenced strings, local variables, and a confidence-rated behavior summary.

prompt_templates.title: Find Network Behavior
prompt_templates.scenario: Review a binary for communications behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace network-related imports and strings. Map caller chains, likely endpoints, request construction, and any unresolved data-flow questions.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Static Analysis Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a structured reverse-engineering summary after multiple findings.
prompt_templates.prompt: Produce a static analysis report with functions analyzed, call graph highlights, data-flow hypotheses, confidence levels, and next dynamic checks.

output_examples.input: Analyze a stripped ARM firmware component for likely network initialization logic.
output: Function map with candidate initialization and main-loop routines.
Cross-reference summary showing calls into socket, connect, send, and receive wrappers.
Hypothesis list with confidence levels and recommended dynamic checks.

output_examples.input: Explain what one decompiled function appears to do.
output: Inputs, outputs, important strings, and called APIs.
Uncertainties caused by missing symbols, indirect calls, or incomplete decompilation.

output_examples.input: Document static evidence for cryptographic behavior in a binary.
output: Relevant crypto imports and nearby cross-references.
Candidate key material or buffer handling locations.
Notes separating confirmed facts from hypotheses.

best_practices: Analyze untrusted binaries in an isolated environment with explicit approval before any execution.
Record facts, hypotheses, confidence levels, and command sources separately.
Start with broad enumeration, then decompile only the functions that answer the investigation question.
anti_patterns: Running unknown binaries natively before static triage and approval.
Using deep analysis on large binaries before narrowing the target functions.
Treating decompiler output as ground truth without checking disassembly and cross-references.

faq.question: Does this skill execute binaries automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and repeatedly notes that execution requires human approval.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It mainly expects radare2, r2ghidra, Ghidra headless, jq, grep, and optional QEMU or Docker.

faq.question: Can it analyze malware safely?
faq.answer: It can support static malware triage, but samples should stay in an isolated analysis environment.

faq.question: Does it require source code?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for compiled binaries, stripped binaries, and firmware components.

faq.question: Can it recover perfect source code?
faq.answer: No. Decompilation is approximate and must be validated against disassembly and runtime evidence.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it guides external command execution and analysis of potentially hostile binaries.
